content:
This was supposed to be a tech blog. Somewhere along the line it became something else, and I think we both know whose fault that is. I write these because she makes me, and she somehow enjoys reading my reflections on her, written about her, largely for her, in what I can only describe as an awkward third person that we’ve both just agreed to pretend is normal. So here we are again.
I’ve been thinking about thinking about her, which is its own strange loop to be stuck in. And I’ve been thinking about love, what it actually is, what people mistake it for, and why the word itself feels completely inadequate for what I’m trying to describe.
Relationships are social constructs. That’s not a cynical thing to say, it’s just true. The labels we put on people, boyfriend, girlfriend, partner, just friends, it’s all language we invented to approximate something that doesn’t fit neatly into boxes. What I have doesn’t fit neatly into a box. There isn’t a word for it that doesn’t either undersell it or misrepresent it entirely, so I’ve stopped looking for one.
What I notice, looking around at the relationships people my age throw themselves into, is that a lot of what gets called love is really just the performance of love. It’s showy. It’s loud. It burns bright and fast and then it’s gone, three months later, and they’re onto the next one having apparently loved each other completely throughout. I don’t say that to be unkind. Puppy love is real in its own way. But it’s romanticised to the point where it collapses under its own weight the moment the world pushes back even slightly.
Because love is not showing off. Love is showing up. It’s being present and actually listening, not as a performance but as a default. It’s the quiet, unglamorous business of caring about someone consistently, not just when it’s easy or exciting or new.
I think about fireworks a lot when I think about this. A firework is genuinely beautiful. It lights up the whole sky, everyone looks, everyone gasps. And then it’s gone. Ten seconds, maybe. Nothing left but smoke.
A candle is a different thing entirely. There’s nothing showy about a candle. But there’s the gentle flicker of the orange flame, and the smell of the burning wick, and the small amount of warmth it throws into a room, and it lasts for years if you look after it properly. The thing is, you have to learn to appreciate a candle differently. You have to be close enough to notice it. You have to actually look.
And here’s the thing people miss. Both have power, just released differently. A candle can light a firework. The firework is brilliant and loud and everyone sees it, but it needed the candle to exist at all. And as long as the candle is never extinguished, it can light countless fireworks over a lifetime. The people who are only the firework get one brilliant moment and then they’re smoke.
But find someone who is also a candle, and something else happens entirely. Two candles, steady and constant, lighting each other’s fireworks. Every brilliant moment between them exists because the flame was already there, patient and quiet, waiting. And because neither of them burns out, there’s no limit to how many of those moments there can be. Just two people, a lifetime of fireworks, and the candle still going at the end of it.
People tell me I’m boring. That I’m missing out, that I should be out there making a mess of things with a rotating cast of people I’ll have forgotten by summer. And maybe to them it looks like I’m standing still. But I’ve just figured out how I want to spend my time, and more importantly, who I want to spend it with. That’s not boring. That’s just knowing what you have.
She is not mine in any possessive sense. But she is my person. There’s a difference, and it matters.
My person is the one I can’t wait to tell things to. Not the big things necessarily, just things. Something funny, something strange, something that happened in the corridor that was so outrageous it was almost impressive. She’s the first place my brain goes when something happens worth sharing, which is most things, because apparently everything is worth sharing when it’s her. She’s the one I’m still thinking about when I’m trying to shut my brain down for the night, which if you know me at all you’ll know is not a quick process under the best of circumstances. She makes it slower and I don’t mind even slightly.
She is my stability. Not in a way that puts any of that on her, that’s not fair to anyone, but in the sense that she is a constant I have built around quietly and without really deciding to. The world is loud and things fall apart and people disappoint you, and none of it feels quite as heavy when she’s there. What we have is stable in the way that actually useful things are stable. Not rigid, not loveless, not boring. Just grounded. Rational in the way that a decision you’ve thought through properly is rational. Controlled not because the feeling is small, but because we’re both sensible enough to hold it carefully.
With love, Daniel
summary: This was supposed to be a tech blog. Somewhere along the line it became something else, and I think we both know whose fault that is. I write these because she makes me, and she somehow enjoys reading my reflections on her, written about her, largely for her, in what I can only describe as an awkward third person that we’ve both just agreed to pretend is normal. So here we are again.
title: On Love, Actually

content:
Right. So. It’s been a while.
A few months of radio silence across basically everything. The blog, the socials, all of it. Life got in the way in the way it sometimes does and somewhere along the line I just stopped. Not intentionally, it just sort of happened. But I’m back, and I had a pretty packed weekend, so let’s get into it.
This weekend has been a funny mix of making money and spending it. About a year ago I started making 3D printed morse keys as a little side hustle. Nothing serious, just a way to scrape a bit of cash from the hobby. They were the first thing I ever sold, ever, and there’s something strange and good about making actual money from something you built with your hands. I kept at it for a while then drifted off, as I tend to do. Then out of nowhere this weekend three orders landed at once. Not life changing stuff, about $50 all up, but that’s not really the point. It got me back in the mood for it and that’s worth something on its own.
The timing also helped, because I’d just had to drop $300 I really didn’t want to spend on a new laptop after the ThinkPad met its end on a physics classroom floor. I was saving for a Vespa. A rubbish $1000 one, sure, but a Vespa nonetheless. The keys coming back at exactly this moment felt like the universe doing me a small favour, and I’ll take it.
While all that was going on I built a microphone for a QMX, knocked out a 3D printed colour coded cable organiser as a little design job, and took on a Kenwood TS-50 recap. The TS-50 came down from a guy in Christchurch who sent it to me specifically, which is always a nice bit of trust to have placed in you. These radios are old enough now that the capacitors are well past their best, so I pulled it apart and replaced the lot. It went back better than it arrived, which is true, but I’ll be honest, it’s still not quite right. There’s something in there I haven’t fully chased down yet. Better than it was, not as good as it should be. We’ll see if it comes back.
And then there’s the Ender 6, sitting in pieces at Zero Waste right now. Zero Waste is the shop I volunteer at where we take in donated tech and restore it instead of letting it go to landfill. The plan with the Ender 6 is to get it running properly and use it at repair cafes, printing whatever parts are needed to keep things out of the bin. It’s a CoreXY machine, theoretically faster and more capable than your average printer once it’s dialled in. I say theoretically because right now it very much is not dialled in, and I’m looking forward to every frustrating minute of getting it there.
Working on it reminded me of fixing the da Vinci 1.0A, getting on for four years ago now. That machine had no business being as much fun as it was. It was a terrible printer and I loved it completely. The Ender 6 has that same energy. But the da Vinci memory doesn’t just stop at the printer. It was also around that time that I met the first person who I just knew wasn’t going anywhere. People come and go, that’s just the nature of things, but every now and then someone comes along and something in your brain quietly goes oh. This one’s different. This one’s staying.
Which got me thinking about love as a concept. Not the greeting card version, the actual thing. I said to someone the other day that love and hate are equal magnitude vectors pointing in opposite directions. You can’t hate something that intensely without caring about it just as deeply. The intensity is the same, just the direction changes, which means in some strange way they’re the same feeling wearing different clothes.
I’m not great with words when it comes to this stuff. I feel things clearly enough, I just don’t always manage to say them out loud. An I hate you from me carries a lot more weight than it probably should, because it usually means the exact opposite, and I think the people who matter understand that. I think it. I imply it constantly. I just haven’t always been able to say it plainly. I’m working on that, slowly closing the gap between what I feel and what I actually articulate. This blog is part of that, if I’m honest. Writing things down and putting them somewhere outside my own head.
Oh, the joys of thinking like me. Can’t even look at a printer without ending up here. And yes, quite literally, when I say I hate you, I quite literally mean the opposite. I would very much like to just say it. But it would also be a bit unnerving if british boy suddenly gained the ability to process emotions. These posts wander all over the place, from capacitors to philosophy to whatever this is, but they all end up in the same place, written for the same person. This one’s no different.
With love, Daniel

title: Back On the Air

content:
Nothing thrilling at all with this one, but if it’s visible after 5:40pm on 23/11/25 NZST then I have successfully implemented a much more automated workflow. This means I can write as much as I want, and literally push one button which runs a .bat script that pushes it to Github after prompting me only for a commit message. My server then has another script which hopefully runs every 20 minutes past the hour (3 times an hour, at :00, :20, and :40) if I wrote it correctly that should delete the old directory and automagically pull the updated copy from Github.
That’s all for now, this is just a test of my automation.
Update 6:22pm, the server-side cronjob isn’t running. Must have scripted it wrong somehow. I’m honestly more inclined to make a comically large physical update button at this point… The upside is now everything is technically automated, or at least I have to think less and I can just run two scripts to upload a new update.

title: Server testing

content:
3D Printing Resources A running list of the most useful stuff I’ve found while trying to get prints to not look like cursed spaghetti. Whether you’re calibrating, troubleshooting, or just figuring out what the hell retraction actually does — these are worth checking out.
🧠 Guides & Knowledge Bases Prusa Knowledge Base
Fantastic tutorials, calibration help, slicer explanations — even if you don’t use Prusa gear.
Teaching Tech Calibration Guides
Absolute goldmine for dialling in everything from e-steps to input shaping.
Cura Docs
If you’re using Cura, start here before guessing at random settings.
🛠 Forums & Help Prusa Forum
Super active and helpful — even the angry rants usually include solutions.
r/FixMyPrint
Post your disasters, get actual feedback. Sometimes even kind feedback.
r/3Dprinting
Big community, good for finding answers, inspiration, or just seeing what other people are breaking. (or being told “bambu lab just works” :P)
📺 YouTube Channels Teaching Tech
Practical, clear, and surprisingly in-depth.
CHEP / Filament Friday
Cura tips, Ender 3 fixes, and printable test files.
CNC Kitchen
Print strength testing, slicing experiments, and lots of “why” behind the “what.”
Got a resource I missed? Shoot it through — or better yet, let me break my printer trying it and write about it here later.
summary:
3D Printing Resources

A running list of the most useful stuff I’ve found while trying to get prints to not look like cursed spaghetti. Whether you’re calibrating, troubleshooting, or just figuring out what the hell retraction actually does — these are worth checking out.

🧠 Guides & Knowledge Bases

  • Prusa Knowledge Base
    Fantastic tutorials, calibration help, slicer explanations — even if you don’t use Prusa gear.

  • Teaching Tech Calibration Guides
    Absolute goldmine for dialling in everything from e-steps to input shaping.
title: 3D Printing Resources
